Regime Shifts in Executive Compensation*
Franklin Allen† Archishman Chakraborty‡
Bhagwan Chowdhry§
Nishant Ravi ¶
This version: December 2025
Abstract
We study optimal compensation when shareholders observe neither the manager's feasible
action set nor his chosen action. Such hidden information induces regime shifts in compensation
within a competitive matching market of firms and managers. Because managerial talent is
portable, equilibrium wages feed back into firms' regime choices, generating discontinuous pay
hikes, abrupt changes in firm volatility, and weak links between pay and performance. Com-
pensation can rise above outside options for all managers, even when (potential) managers are
abundant. Thus, we explain several well-documented empirical regularities, using a competitive
model with a single within-match friction, and no rent seeking by managers.
JEL classification: D82, D86, G34.
Key Words: executive compensation, hidden information, hidden action, assortative match-
ing, regime shifts.
***
*We thank Gabriela Coiculescu, Henry Friedman, Dilip Mookherjee, Ivo Welch and seminar participants at Bran-
deis University, University of Miami, Yeshiva University and UCLA for useful comments and suggestions.
†Imperial College London, f.allen@imperial.ac.uk.
‡Syms School, Yeshiva University, archishman@yu.edu.
§Indian School of Business, bhagwan@isb.edu.
¶Indian School of Business, nishant_ravi@isb.edu.

[Page 2]
# 1 Introduction
CEO compensation is contentious, particularly in times of recessions, economic crises and increasing inequality. Piketty and Saez (2014) document that the share of income going to the top 10% has been increasing in the past few decades, reaching nearly 50% in the United States and 35% in Europe. They show that “a very large part of the rise in the top 10% income share comes from the top 1% (or even the top 0.1%). This is largely due to the rise of top executive compensation in large U.S. corporations (both financial and non-financial).”

The phenomenal rise in CEO compensation in the last few decades is also documented in Frydman (2019), Frydman and Jenter (2010), Kaplan and Rauh (2013), and Edmans, Gabaix and Jenter (2017). In 1980, CEO pay was on average 40 times the pay of the median worker, a ratio that had grown to over 300 by 2015 in the United States. Since 1980, CEO pay in S&P 500 companies has grown 600%.¹

The popular account of what has caused CEO pay to rise dramatically is the “rent extraction” view which argues that contracts are effectively set by executives themselves to maximize their own rents. In this view, the compensation process is rigged. On the contrary, the “shareholder value” view argues that compensation contracts are chosen to maximize value for shareholders, taking into account the competitive market for executives and the need to provide adequate incentives (Edmans, Gabaix and Jenter, 2017). This paper belongs to the second school. Yet, we show why competitive outcomes may look rigged. High equilibrium levels of managerial pay can lead to regime changes by value-maximizing firms that result in significantly higher levels of incentive pay but lower shareholder value.

We propose a model of competitive matching between firms and managers under within-firm agency conflicts. The within-firm frictions are of the hidden information and hidden action variety, and managerial skills are portable across firms.² Our results are consistent with three important empirical observations.

1. Before 1980, CEO compensation remained stable relative to several benchmarks but then there was a regime shift and CEO earnings increased dramatically, as did firm volatility.
2. Optimal CEO pay can be high even when the stock price is low.
3. The least talented manager’s compensation can be high, leading to widespread pay escalation due to competition, even with an excess supply of potential managers.

---
¹Not surprisingly, wealth inequality is even more stark (Piketty and Saez, 2014) while consumption inequality is much less so (McCloskey, 2014).

²By portability, we mean the opposite of firm-specific skills, i.e., general human capital that remains productive across many firms (see, e.g., Murphy and Zábojník 2004).

[Page 3]
A number of academic scholars have argued that extremely high CEO pay could arise if managerial talent is scarce but portable across firms (Gabaix and Landier, 2008; Kaplan and Rauh, 2013, Edmans and Gabaix, 2016). When CEOs are incentivized to increase the value of the equity they hold—typically around 0.3-0.4% in large firms—this relatively small ownership stake can have a disproportionately large impact on firm value, particularly in very large firms that compete for a limited pool of highly talented CEOs. Edmans, Gabaix and Jenter (2017) show that the large increase in CEO pay from the 1980’s to early 2000’s mirrors the increase in size of the largest firms in the U.S. Although total pay has risen for firms of all sizes, the increases were steeper in larger firms. They caution, however, that “while the level of pay has generally increased over time, this trend has been neither constant nor uniform, contrary to popular belief.” Like Frydman and Saks (2010), they note that the CEO compensation levels from World War II to 1970 were remarkably flat, even though firms grew considerably during that time. Clearly, something fundamental changed in the 1980s. This is our first empirical observation above. We explore this key issue, while studying returns to managerial talent.

Edmans, Gabaix and Jenter (2017) also suggest that for “future research, it would be interesting to model the trade-off between inducing executives to take ‘good’ risks and discouraging them from taking ‘bad’ risks.” We take up exactly this suggestion. We show that regime changes in the risk profile of firms are jointly determined with widespread escalation in executive pay, which not only explains the timing of pay escalation after the 1980s but also generates several additional testable implications about the types of firms for which we are likely to see pay escalation.

We start by formalizing the trade-off between good and bad risks in an agency model with two key informational frictions. One, shareholders cannot monitor the CEO’s decisions (a hidden action problem), which matters because the CEO has personal preferences that are not perfectly aligned with those of the firm’s shareholders (because of a preference for power, media recognition, socially responsible projects, etc.). Two, shareholders cannot fully observe the set of opportunities actually available to the firm (a hidden information problem): the manager knows whether or not a valuable but risky growth opportunity is available, but shareholders do not.

To incentivize the CEO to undertake the valuable growth opportunity whenever available, an optimal contract must offer high-powered incentives. But such incentives have a perverse effect: when the growth opportunity is unavailable, the CEO may be tempted by the contract to undertake a different, value-destroying risky action that looks similar to a value-maximizing one (Brown, Harlow, and Starks, 1996). To prevent this, the contract must also handsomely compensate the CEO for choosing a safe, low-risk project whenever better options are not available. The hidden information problem thus creates a need to provide strong incentives in both states of the world—when a good growth opportunity is available and when it is not—driving the average level of compensation

[Page 4]
to be quite high, regardless of the likelihood of good growth opportunities. So, when the expected
benefit from pursuing growth is not large enough, it is optimal for the firm to forego growth and
pay the manager less.

The market may infer information about the decisions taken by the manager. When it learns
that value-increasing growth opportunities have likely been undertaken, the stock price will rise.
Otherwise, the stock price will fall. If the manager gets higher private benefits (or pays lower
private costs) from decisions that generate lower expected cash flows, managerial compensation
will be positively correlated with firm performance. But if the manager is partially aligned with
shareholders, with higher private benefits from taking decisions that yield riskier cash flows (good
or bad), managerial compensation will be negatively correlated with firm performance.

The contracting problem thus provides an explanation of the evidence that CEO pay is not
tightly related to firm performance, which is our second empirical observation above. Bertrand
and Mullainathan (2001) find that executives are paid for firm performance that might be driven
by good performance of the entire sector, largely unrelated to managerial actions. This result
may not be surprising in an efficient market in which investors properly anticipate the expected
effects of managerial actions (Holmstrom, 1979; Murphy, 2013). However, Garvey and Milbourn
(2006) document a suspicious asymmetry in CEO pay CEO pay is high following positive sector
performance ("good luck") but CEO pay does not appear to be sufficiently low following negative
sector performance (“bad luck"). This is one of the main pieces of evidence for the “rent extraction"
view that the compensation process is rigged. It has led to concerns that entrenched managers may
have captured corporate boards and manipulated the pay-setting process to their own advantage
(Bebchuk and Fried, 2003; see Kuhnen and Zwiebel, 2009 for a model of compensation under
entrenchment). We show that even value-maximizing shareholders may set optimal compensation
contracts that reward CEOs for luck, with high compensation accompanied by a low stock price.
Our model is therefore consistent with the second empirical observation above.

We next investigate how our contracting problem affects two-sided matching between managers
of heterogeneous talent with firms of varying quality. Talent and quality are complementary inputs
into the expected value of the risky but valuable growth opportunity. We first characterize the set
of stable matchings in balanced markets, where the two sides of the market are of the same total
size. We show that all managers rank stable matchings in the same way, as do firms. But the
two common rankings on the two sides of the market are exactly opposed. The manager-optimal
(stable) matching is the one that is the best for all managers, while the firm-optimal (stable)
matching is the one that is most preferred by all firms (i.e., shareholders). Demand for talent is
the highest possible in the former and the lowest possible in the latter.

An unbalanced market, where the two sides are not of equal size, has a unique stable matching.

[Page 5]
When there are more potential managers than firms, we look at the balanced market that is
created by keeping the excess mass of the least talented managers unmatched. We show that
the unique equilibrium is the firm-optimal matching of this balanced market, with wages driven
down by the mass of unmatched managers. However, under an aggregate scarcity of managers, the
unique equilibrium selects the manager-optimal stable matching of the balanced market created by
keeping the excess mass of the lowest quality firms unmatched. The two matching outcomes differ
significantly in the level and distribution of executive pay.

In the firm-optimal matching, only high-quality firms, for whom undertaking risky growth is
a dominant strategy, pursue such opportunities by attracting top managerial talent with high
incentive pay. Other lower-quality firms, for whom the probability of encountering the growth
opportunity is too remote, prefer to forego risky growth and hire less talented managers at (dis-
continuously) lower pay. In the manager-optimal matching, in contrast, all firms choose to pursue
risky growth opportunities, no matter how unlikely they are to materialize, forced entirely by the
equilibrium pressures of high managerial pay to do so.

If managers could collectively enforce a stable matching, they would pick the manager-optimal
one. But such managerial capture of the pay process is not necessary for high executive pay.
Competitive pressures generated by slight imbalances in the total supply of managers versus firms
are enough to select one or the other stable matching, resulting in large differences in managerial
pay, firm volatility, and total surplus. In fact, it is the firm-optimal matching that has a flavor of
rent extraction, since total surplus is (at least weakly) lower in each firm, and shareholders get a
larger share of this smaller pie. The manager-optimal matching maximizes the total pie.

These results provide one explanation for the historical evidence of a dramatic escalation in ex-
ecutive pay since the 1980s. Technological progress, productivity growth, financial market changes,
and regulatory reforms may have eased entry into the market for potential firms. Absent a corre-
sponding growth in the supply of potential managers, perhaps because of social constraints or the
high costs of education and training, one should expect a cascade of regime changes across firms
over time, and an attendant large escalation in executive pay.³ An improvement in the quality
distribution of firms, or a worsening of the talent distribution of managers, would further add to
the pay escalation while lowering payoffs on the firm side.

A scarcity in the total supply of potential managers is not necessary for widespread escalation
in executive pay. If sufficiently many (of even the less talented) managers have good enough out-
side options, there could be a *relative* scarcity of managers for lower quality firms that seek to

³Limited competition in a monopolistic education market, that keeps the price of education high and the quantity
educated low, may therefore have an important role in creating high executive pay and, ultimately, high income
inequality.

[Page 6]
forego the risky growth regime and pay the manager less. Just like a market with an aggregate
shortage of potential managers, this relative scarcity could also lead to a cascade of regime changes,
economy-wide pay escalation, and large managerial rents in the unique equilibrium, even though
some potential managers remain unmatched. Alternative well-compensated employment opportu-
nities in the private equity and hedge fund industries, as well as entrepreneurial tech opportunities,
that grew explosively more or less at the same time as the observed escalation in executive compen-
sation, may have provided the required stimulus to managerial outside options (Gompers, Kaplan
and Mukharlyamov, 2024). This feature of our model—that low-quality managers can be highly
compensated even though some potential managers remain unmatched—is consistent with our third
empirical observation that all managerial pay has increased, even that of the less-talented ones.

In addition to rationalizing compensation patterns over time, our framework generates a rich
set of sharp, testable cross-sectional predictions. It provides a causal mechanism for the empirically
documented correlation between CEO pay and volatility, predicting that high compensation and
high firm risk are jointly determined.⁴ More pointedly, our model suggests that large, discontinuous
jumps in CEO pay should be observed primarily in firms that undergo a “regime shift” from
safe, low-growth strategies to risky, high-growth ones. Hidden information frictions are key to
this insight. The shift to a high-pay/high-risk regime can optimally occur with no corresponding
increase in shareholder value, explaining the often-puzzling disconnect between pay hikes and firm
performance. The cascade effect described above provides a market-based explanation for the
widespread and rapid nature of pay increases post-1980s. It also implies that the compensation for
skilled CEOs was much more sensitive to their ability post-1980s, compared to the relatively flat
CEO compensation before the 1970s (Frydman and Saks, 2010).⁵

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 analyzes the contracting problem with a
fixed firm and manager. Section 3 endogenizes the firm-manager pairing via a competitive market
with two-sided heterogeneity. Section 4 provides a discussion including testable empirical predic-
tions. Section 5 concludes. The related literature is discussed at appropriate points throughout the
paper. The Appendix contains all proofs as well as additional robustness results and extensions.

# 2 A Contracting Problem

A principal of a firm (the shareholders collectively) must design a compensation contract for an agent (the CEO or manager) at date 0. Because of informational frictions and agency conflicts that we detail below, the compensation contract must incentivize the manager to engage in shareholder value-maximizing activities. The manager's choices affect the distribution of future cash flows, and his compensation is contingent on these cash flows. All agents are risk-neutral, and the risk-free rate is normalized to zero.

After accepting his contract on date 0, the CEO faces a choice among (at most) three alternatives. He can take a "safe" decision, $d = S$, that generates cash flows of \$1 at date 1. He can also take a "bad" decision, $d = B$, that yields cash flows on date 2 equal to $R$ w.p. $p_B$ and $r$ w.p. $1 - p_B$, where $R > 1 > r \ge 0$ and $p_B \in (0,1)$. Furthermore, w.p. $\phi \in (0,1)$, it is feasible for the CEO to take a "good" decision, $d = G$, that is similar to $B$, except that the probability of receiving the high cash cash flow $R$ is higher, $p_G \in (p_B, 1]$. With probability $1 - \phi$, the decision $G$ is not feasible and the manager must choose between $S$ and $B$. We think of $S$ as a safe project, while $B$ and $G$ are risky growth options. Let $V_S = 1$, and for $d = B, G$, let $V_d = p_d R + (1 - p_d)r$. Assume $V_G > V_S > V_B$.

If the shareholders could enforce a decision, they would make the manager choose $G$ when feasible, and $S$ otherwise. However, the shareholders face a *hidden action* problem, in that they cannot observe the decision $d$ taken by the manager. Furthermore, they face a *hidden information* problem in that they do not observe whether $d = G$ is feasible or not. The two informational frictions together imply that shareholders do not know the feasible set of decisions available to the manager and which decision the manager actually took from his feasible set. They need to incentivize the manager to take the decisions they desire from a set of feasible decisions that is unknown to them. Assume that cash flows are verifiable in a court so that the compensation of the manager can be made contingent on realized cash flows.

To make incentive provision non-trivial, assume the manager earns (non-pecuniary) private benefit $b_d$ from decision $d \in \{S, B, G\}$ and normalize $b_S = 0$. Assume $b_B > \max\{0, b_G\}$. These private incentives may arise out of empire-building motives on the part of the manager or because of behavioral biases such as hubris or overconfidence.⁶ In the case $b_G < 0$, the manager's ranking of his private benefits from each decision is the opposite of the shareholders' ranking of total cash flows. Such directly opposed interests correspond to the standard cost of effort formulation of hidden action moral hazard problems. Generating higher expected cash flows involves more costly

---
⁶The model permits ready analysis of other kinds of agency conflicts, such as preference for a quiet life ($b_G < b_B < b_S$). We do not include this analysis for reasons of space.

[Page 8]
effort on the part of the manager, resulting in lower private benefits. In contrast, when $b_G > 0$, the
manager's private incentives are partially conflicted with shareholders' but also partially aligned.
We assume that
$$
V_B + b_B < 1 < V_G + b_G,
\quad (1)
$$
i.e., that the ranking of cash flows coincides with the ranking of the total pie, including private ben-
efits. This implies that, absent any informational frictions, the shareholders would like the manager
to pick the growth option G when feasible and the safe option S otherwise. We use the symbol g
to denote this first-best regime (or decision rule). As we show below, the informational frictions
described above may sometimes force the shareholders to incentivize the manager to implement
the regime ng that foregoes the growth option and always picks the safe decision S.

Let $w_1$ denote managerial compensation at date 1 when the realized cash flow is 1. Let $w_2$
denote managerial compensation at date 2 when cash flows are $r$, and $w_2 + W_2$ the compensation
when cash flows are $R$. Thus, $W_2$ is the bonus the manager receives for high cash flows. Let
$W = (w_1, w_2, W_2)$ denote a compensation contract. At date 0, W must guarantee an expected
utility of at least $u \ge 0$, the exogenous outside option of the manager.⁷ We restrict attention to
the case where $u < 1$ so that it is profitable for the shareholders to implement the regime ng (and
so also g), absent any informational problems.

The manager has limited liability, i.e., he must receive non-negative payments. We assume that
the firm has enough cash to pay the manager and, accordingly, ignore limited liability constraints
on the firm side. As mentioned above, shareholders must also provide incentives for the manager
to choose the correct decision in each state of the world. Figure 1 denotes the timing of events.⁸

## 2.1 Inducing growth: the regime g

Suppose the shareholders wish to induce the first-best regime g. The date 0 expected shareholder
value is
$$
\phi(V_G - w_2 - p_G W_2) + (1 - \phi)(1 - w_1)
\quad (2)
$$

---

Suppose the shareholders wish to induce the first-best regime g. The date 0 expected shareholder
value is
$$
\phi(V_G - w_2 - p_G W_2) + (1 - \phi)(1 - w_1)
\quad (2)
$$

---
⁷In the matching market of Section 3, we will endogenize the firm-manager pairing and, consequently, the outside
option u.

⁸We assume that none of the three actions requires an up-front investment. This is harmless as long as financing

assumptions keep the analysis clean.

Figure 1: Timing of events

Shareholders must choose $W = (w_1, w_2, W_2)$ to maximize (2) subject to the following constraints:
$$
w_2 + p_G W_2 + b_G \ge w_2 + p_B W_2 + b_B \quad (3)
$$
$$
w_2 + p_G W_2 + b_G \ge w_1 \quad (4)
$$
$$
w_1 \ge w_2 + p_B W_2 + b_B \quad (5)
$$
$$
\phi(w_2 + p_G W_2 + b_G) + (1 - \phi)w_1 \ge u \quad (6)
$$
$$
w_1, w_2, W_2 + w_2 \ge 0 \quad (7)
$$

The first two constraints, (3) and (4), are hidden action incentive constraints. They ensure, respectively, that the manager prefers to undertake $G$ over $B$ and $G$ over $S$. Constraint (5) is another incentive constraint that arises out of the hidden information problem. It ensures the manager prefers $S$ over $B$ (when $G$ is not feasible). Together, the three incentive constraints guarantee that the manager will implement the regime $g$. Constraint (6) is the participation constraint that assures the manager an expected payoff of at least $u$. The limited liability constraints are captured by (7). Let $\omega_g = \frac{p_G b_B - p_B b_G}{p_G - p_B}$. As we show below, $\omega_g$ is the minimum possible payoff to the manager when the firm implements $g$.

**Proposition 1** *Suppose the shareholders wish to implement the first-best regime $g$. Then the date 0 expected shareholder value is*
$$
1 + \phi(V_G - 1 + b_G) - \max\{u, \omega_g\}.
$$

*A compensation contract that achieves this objective at minimum cost is given by $w_1 = \max\{u, \omega_g\}$, $w_2 = \max\{0, u - \omega_g\}$, $W_2 = \frac{b_B - b_G}{p_G - p_B}$.*

The proof of Proposition 1 shows that when $u < \omega_g$, the participation constraint (6) does not bind. Incentive and limited liability constraints determine managerial compensation. The manager's expected payoff is $\omega_g$, which gives him rents over his outside option $u$ even though he

[Page 10]
has no bargaining power and shareholders hold all rights over contracting.⁹ The optimal contract is unique, and a number of its features arise from the interaction of the hidden action and the hidden information problems faced by the shareholders.

First, the bonus $W_2$ is entirely determined by the choice between $G$ and $B$. The more similar are the private benefits of the manager for these two options, the lower is $W_2$; and the more similar $G$ is to $B$ (i.e., the closer $p_g$ is to $p_B$), the higher is $W_2$. This contracting feature creates adverse incentives for the manager: the bonus $W_2$ tempts the manager to choose $B$ over $S$ when $G$ is not feasible. To ensure the manager chooses $S$ when it is the best feasible alternative, $w_1$ must equal $w_g$. Like $W_2$, $w_g$ is larger the more difficult it is to distinguish between $G$ and $B$.

These properties of the optimal contract extend to situations where $u \ge w_g$ when the manager does not earn rents over his outside option, although in this case the cost-minimizing contract is not uniquely pinned down. Any contract that provides an expected payoff of $u$ to the manager and satisfies all the other constraints is an optimal contract. This indeterminacy has no effect on the expected level of managerial compensation and shareholder value. Proposition 1 provides the optimal contract that has the lowest bonus component in the class of all contracts that minimize the wage bill. The expected payoff of the manager equals $\max\{u, w_g\}$ in all cases.¹⁰

Proposition 1 has implications for the correlation between firm performance and executive compensation at date 1. To derive them, suppose that the market observes a signal $\psi \in \{L, H\}$ at date 1 that is correlated with the arrival of the valuable growth option $G$. Assume that
$$
\text{Pr}[\psi = H|G \text{ arrives}] = \text{Pr}[\psi = L|G \text{ does not arrive}] = \rho \in (\frac{1}{2}, 1]. \quad (8)
$$
The parameter $\rho$ measures the informativeness of the signal $\psi$. Denote by $P_{1,\psi}$ the date 1 stock price (or shareholder value) as a function of $\psi$. This equals expected total surplus less expected managerial compensation, given $\psi$. Denote by $E[W|\psi]$ the expected value of the managerial compensation package as a function of the signal $\psi$. Our next result shows that the random variables $P_{1,\psi}$ and $E[W|\psi]$ may display arbitrary correlation patterns, depending on the underlying agency conflict between the manager and shareholders.¹¹

¹⁰Since the manager knows whether $G$ arrives or not, one may wonder if the shareholders can do better by making contracts contingent on a message from the manager that reveals this information. [Page 11]
**Proposition 2** Suppose the shareholders wish to implement the first-best regime g. If $b_G > 0$, $E[W|\psi]$ is negatively correlated with $P_{1,\psi}$. If $b_G < 0$, $E[W|\psi]$ and $P_{1,\psi}$ are positively correlated, while if $b_G = 0$ they are uncorrelated.

When the shareholders induce g, and $b_G > 0$, the manager is partially aligned with the shareholder in that he is more motivated towards choosing G over S, all else held equal. Good news about shareholder value, in the form of the likely arrival of the valuable growth opportunity G, is then also a signal of lower conflict of interest between the manager and shareholders. So his expected optimal compensation can be smaller when $\psi = H$, compared to when $\psi = L$ and the manager is more likely to face a choice between S and B. The date 1 value of the pay package will be negatively correlated with shareholder value. In contrast, when $b_G < 0$, the manager's private incentives are directly opposed to shareholder interests. So the stock price and expected value of the pay package must display a positive correlation.

## 2.2 Optimal regimes

So far, we have derived the optimal contract assuming that shareholders implement the first best regime g. We now provide the optimal contract when the shareholders decide to forego any growth option and induce the manager to always choose the safe option S. We call this regime ng. If implemented, the expected date 0 shareholder value is $1 - w_1$. The shareholders choose $W = (w_1, w_2, W_2)$ to maximize this subject to the following constraints
$$
w_1 \ge w_2 + p_B W_2 + b_B
$$
$$
w_1 \ge w_2 + p_G W_2 + b_G
$$
$$
w_1 \ge u
$$
$$
w_1, w_2, W_2 \ge 0
$$
The first two constraints are incentive constraints that ensure that the safe action is better for the manager than either the bad or the good action. The third constraint is the participation constraint, while the last one is the limited liability constraint. It is easy to check that a cost-minimizing way to implement ng is to set deferred compensation $w_2 = W_2 = 0$, and $w_1 = \max\{u, w_{ng}\}$ where, by analogy with Proposition 1, $w_{ng} \equiv b_B$ is the minimum managerial expected payoff (in this case, efficiency wage) associated with the regime ng.

Note that $w_{ng} < w_g$. It is less costly to provide incentives in regime ng than in regime g. This is firstly because the hidden information problem has any bite only in the latter case, in which the contract itself creates monetary incentives to take value-destroying decisions that must be mitigated by providing additional incentive pay. This implies $w_g$ is independent of the probability $\phi$ with

him to chase relatively rare growth opportunities. This feature is a key ingredient for our results

# 3 The Matching Market

We now embed our contracting problem into a model of frictionless matching between multiple
(potential) managers and firms. Managers differ in talent, firms in quality. Talent and quality are
observable and complementary inputs into the production of total surplus within a manager-firm
pair. In particular, talent is not firm-specific: it is portable across firms in a manner that depends
on firm quality.

As is well known, complementarities often generate increasing returns to managerial talent (and
firm quality) through positively assortative matching. As we show below, the two informational
frictions in our contracting problem—hidden action and hidden information—additionally generate
regime shifts within and across firms, leading to pay escalation accompanied by changes in firm
characteristics. Within-firm hidden information, in particular, is the key driver of these regime
shifts. The presence of both frictions distinguishes our analysis within the related literature on
matching with endogenous contracting. This literature either abstracts from informational frictions
(Becker 1973; Legros and Newman 2007; Gabaix and Landier 2008; Thanassoulis 2012; Chiappori
and Reny 2016; Gierlinger and Laczó 2018), or focuses on asymmetric information about talent
(Terviö 2008; Eisfeldt and Kuhnen 2013), or treats hidden action as the sole friction (Dam and
Pérez-Castrillo 2006; von Lilienfeld-Toal and Mookherjee 2010; Alonso-Paulí and Pérez-Castrillo
2012; Ghatak and Karaivanov 2014; Antón and Dam 2020; Liu 2024).[^12]

Let $f \in [0, \bar{f}]$ index a firm and $m \in [0, \bar{m}]$ index a manager. Let $P$ and $Q$ be the cumulative
distribution of firms and managers, respectively, with strictly positive densities $p$ and $q$. Set $P(\bar{f}) = \gamma > 0$ and $Q(\bar{m}) = 1$. When $\gamma = 1$, the market is *balanced*; otherwise it is *unbalanced*.

Firms and managers match in pairs. The characteristics of a match $(f, m)$ determine the
probability $\phi(f, m)$ of encountering the good growth option G. We assume $\phi$ is increasing and
differentiable in each argument and supermodular.[^13] Thus, manager $m$ is more talented than
$m' < m$ and firm $f$ has higher quality than $f' < f$. Further, talent and quality are complements—a
more talented manager will make a greater difference at a higher quality firm.

[^12]: Limited payoff transferability also distinguishes our setting from the literature on matching under non-transferable utilities initiated by Gale and Shapley (1962).
[^13]: Formally, $\phi$ is supermodular if $\phi(m \vee m', f \vee f') + \phi(m \wedge m', f \wedge f') \ge \phi(m, f) + \phi(m', f')$. When $\phi$ is twice differentiable, this is equivalent to assuming $\phi_{12} \ge 0$ (Topkis, 1998). Supermodularity is a standard assumption in the literature on matching (see, e.g., Becker, 1973; Chade, Eeckhout and Smith, 2017).

[Page 14]
A match $(f, m) \in [0, \bar{f}] \times [0, \bar{m}]$ implements a regime $\theta(f, m) \in \{\text{g, ng}\}$ that delivers an expected payoff of $u_\theta(f,m)$ to the manager, $v_\theta(f, m)$ to the firm, with a total pie $\pi_\theta(f,m) = u_\theta(f,m) + v_\theta(f, m)$.¹⁴ To implement $\theta$, firm $f$ and manager $m$ write contracts satisfying the appropriate limited liability and incentive constraints, like the ones used to establish Propositions 1 through 3. Thus, $\pi_\text{g}(f,m) = 1+\phi(f,m)(V_G - 1+b_g)$, while $\pi_\text{ng}(f, m) = 1$. Let $\Delta_\pi(f, m) := \pi_\text{g}(f, m) – \pi_\text{ng}(f,m) = \kappa\phi(f,m)$ be the expected increase in the pie from implementing g in the match $(f,m)$, where $\kappa := V_G - 1 + b_g$.

A matching rule $\mu$ is a measurable one-to-one function $\mu : [0, \bar{m}] \to [0, \bar{f}] \cup \{-1\}$ that specifies the firm $\mu(m) \in [0, \bar{f}]$ that manager $m$ is matched with, or, if $m$ is unmatched, that $\mu(m) = -1$. Let $\mu^{-1}(f)$ denote the match of a firm $f$, with $\mu^{-1}(f) = -1$ if $f$ is unmatched, i.e., if $f \in [0, \bar{f}]\setminus\mu([0, \bar{m}])$. The rule $\mu$ must be measure-consistent, i.e., $\int_A dP = \int_{\mu^{-1}(A)} dQ$ for any measurable set $A \subseteq [0, \bar{f}]$.¹⁵

A matching is a tuple $(\mu,\theta,u,v)$ that consists of a matching rule $\mu$, and for every match $(\mu(m), m)$, $\mu(m) \neq -1$, a regime $\theta(\mu(m),m)$ that generates expected payoffs $u(\mu(m),m)$ and $v(\mu(m), m)$ to, respectively, the manager $m$ and his matched firm $\mu(m)$. An unmatched manager $m$ gets a payoff $u_0$, while an unmatched firm gets a payoff $v_0$. We further impose that if the market is balanced, and all managers are matched, then all firms are also matched, and vice-versa.¹⁶

A matching $(\mu, \theta, u, v)$ is individually rational if $u_\theta(\mu(m), m) \ge u_0$ and $v_\theta(f, \mu^{-1}(f)) \ge v_0$ for all $m$ and $f$ respectively. A pair $(f,m)$ blocks the matching $(\mu, \theta, u, v)$ if there exists a regime $\theta'$ that generates payoffs $u_{\theta'}(f,m) \ge u_\theta(\mu(m), m)$ and $v_{\theta'}(f, m) \ge v_\theta(f, \mu^{-1}(f))$, with at least one strict inequality. A matching $(\mu, \theta, u, v)$ is stable if it is individually rational and cannot be blocked by any $(f,m)$.

Our equilibrium notion is of a stable matching. We look for the set of all equilibria. For expositional ease, we restrict attention to environments with the following parameter restrictions:¹⁷

R1: $\phi(0,0) > 0$; $\phi(\bar{f},\bar{m}) > \phi^*(0)$.

R2: $b_B > u_0 > 0$.

R3: $\pi_\text{ng}(0,0) - \omega_\text{ng} > \pi_\text{g}(0,0) - \omega_\text{g} > v_0$.

---
¹⁴ When the decision rule $\theta(f, m)$ is understood from the description of a match $(f, m)$, we will suppress the subscript and denote expected payoffs and total surplus in the match as $u(f, m)$, $v(f,m)$ and $\pi(f, m)$, respectively.

¹⁵ This makes it impossible for a measure ½ of managers to be matched one-to-one with a measure ⅓ of firms, for example.

¹⁶ This rules out cases where all the agents on one side of the market are matched with a full measure of agents on the other side, with a zero measure of agents remaining unmatched. We allow for blocking coalitions of a zero measure of agents.

¹⁷ These restrictions are not important for our main results, but they reduce the number of cases to consider.

[Page 15]
Restriction R1 says that even the worst possible manager-firm pair encounters the growth opportunity G with positive probability; while the best possible manager-firm pair prefers to implement g regardless of what the manager must be paid. Restriction R2 focuses on the case where the bad option B yields more private benefits than the manager's payoff from being unmatched, $u_0$, which is type-independent and equal to zero. Restriction R3 states that it is acceptable for the lowest quality firm to hire the least talented manager and pay him $w_θ$ to implement $θ ∈ \{g, ng\}$, compared to remaining unmatched. However, the required difference in incentive pay, $w_g - w_{ng}$, to implement g is greater than $Δ_π(0,0)$, the expected increase in the total pie for this match.

Our next result shows that balanced markets have a large multiplicity of stable matchings. To state it concisely, recall the definition of $ϕ^*(u)$ from (9) and let
$$
m̄ := \sup\{x : ϕ(P⁻¹Q(x),x) < ϕ*(0)\}.
\quad (10)
$$
Because of R3, $m̄$ is well-defined. A firm $μ(m) = P⁻¹Q(m)$, that is in a positively assortative match with manager $m ≥ m̄$, must have a match-specific growth probability $ϕ(P⁻¹Q(m),m) ≥ ϕ*(0)$. From Figure 2, it is then easy to see that such a firm prefers to implement g regardless of the manager's outside option. But if $m < m̄$, then $μ(m)$'s preferred regime depends on what the manager must be paid, a fact crucial for our next result.¹⁸

**Proposition 4** *Assume a balanced market. A matching $(μ,θ,u,v)$ is stable if and only if one can find $m^* ∈ [0,m̄]$ such that:*

(i) for $m ≥ m^*$, $μ(m) = P⁻¹Q(m)$, $θ(μ(m), m) = g$, with
$$
u(μ(m), m) = u(μ(m^*), m^*) + κ ∫_{m^*}^m ϕ₂(μ(t), t)dt
\quad (11)
$$

(ii) for $m < m^*$, $μ(m) ∈ [0, P⁻¹Q(m^*)]$, $θ(μ(m), m) = ng$, with
$$
u(μ(m), m) = u(μ(m^*), m^*) – Δ_π(μ(m^*), m^*)
\quad (12)
$$
where $u(μ(m^*), m^*) ∈ [ω_g, π_g(0,0) – v_0]$ if $m^* = 0$, and equal to $ω_g$ otherwise.

Proposition 4 describes expected payoffs to managers in any stable matching. The expected payoff for each matched firm $μ(m)$ equals $v(μ(m),m) = π(μ(m),m) – u(μ(m), m)$. Since the probability $ϕ(f, m)$ is supermodular in its arguments, it is natural to expect positively assortative matching, $μ(m) = P⁻¹Q(m)$, at least for the matches that implement the regime g. Proposition

4 shows this is indeed the case for any manager $m \geq \bar{m}$, who must implement g in a match with $\mu(m) = P^{-1}Q(m)$, in every stable matching.

Things are more nuanced for managers $m < \bar{m}$. If a manager m of this type is matched with firm $\mu(m) = P^{-1}Q(m)$, then $\mu(m)$'s preferred regime depends on what the manager must be paid. Proposition 4 says that each such manager m must implement g in an assortative match with $P^{-1}Q(m)$, in some stable matching. Indeed, any arbitrary cutoff $m^* \in [0, \bar{m}]$, such that $\theta(\mu(m), m) = g$ if and only if $m \geq m^*$, is consistent with a stable matching. The cutoff type $m^*$ of the manager earns expected payoff of at least $\omega_g$ in its match with $\mu(m^*) = P^{-1}Q(m^*)$.¹⁹

Each $m > m^*$ earns an additional return to managerial talent, equal to $\kappa \int_{m^*}^m \phi_2(\mu(t), t)dt$, the sum of the marginal returns to managerial talent of every manager between $m^*$ and m, computed at the equilibrium matching. The corresponding matched firms, $\mu(m) = P^{-1}Q(m)$, $m > m^*$, also earn increasing returns to quality. In contrast, matching is indeterminate for $m < m^*$. Any such manager can match with any firm in $[0, \mu(m^*))$. All these matches implement ng and expected payoffs are not affected either by the talent of the manager or by the quality of his matched firm.²⁰

Figure 3 illustrates Proposition 4. Panels (a) and (b) plot, respectively, the cross-sectional distribution of expected managerial payoffs and of expected firm payoffs, in three different stable matchings. One interior equilibrium has $m* = 0.5$ (green curves). The other two are extreme equilibria, one with $m* = \bar{m}$ (blue curves) and the other with $m* = 0$ and $u(\mu(0),0) = w_g$ (red curves).²¹ In each stable matching, managerial and firm payoffs are constant for $m < m*$ and increasing otherwise. There is a discontinuity in managerial payoffs at the cutoff type $m*$. This manager captures the entire increase in the pie, $\Delta\pi(\mu(m*), m*)$, from implementing g in the match $(\mu(m*), m*)$, which leaves its match $\mu(m*)$ indifferent between implementing g with $m*$, or ng with any $m \le m*$, sustaining the equilibrium.

Notice that any firm $\mu(m)$ that matches with $m \in [m*, \bar{m})$ is, in a sense, hostage to the competitive pressures generated by the portability of managerial talent. If it could bargain with its matched manager in isolation, subject only to meeting the managerial outside option $u_o = 0$, it would prefer to implement ng, for a lower total pie but higher shareholder value (since $\phi(\mu(m),m) < \phi*(0)$; see Figure 2). But the high equilibrium payoffs of their (optimally) matched manager forces these firms to switch regimes and implement g, for a larger total pie but lower shareholder value. These firms would like to hire a less talented manager below $m*$ to implement ng, if they could find such a manager who was cheap enough. But they cannot. The firms that hire these less talented managers must pay them enough, above what is required for incentive provision, in order to prevent them from being poached by better firms.

Figure 3 shows that every type of manager ranks different stable matchings in the same way: the smaller is $m*$, the larger is the expected payoff to any manager m, regardless of the chosen regime in his match. For $m \le m*$, this follows from the fact that the gap, $\Delta\pi(\mu(m*), m*)$, between $m*$'s expected payoff and that of any $m \le m*$, is smaller the smaller is $m*$. So all $m < m*$ are better off in a stable matching with a smaller cutoff type $m*$. On the other hand, since $m*$ earns at least $w_g$ in any stable matching, and each $m > m*$ earns, in addition, $\kappa \int_{m*}^m \phi_2(\mu(t), t)dt$, the sum of the equilibrium marginal returns to managerial talent of every manager between $m*$ and m, these managers also benefit from a smaller $m*$.

Similar arguments show that every type of firm also ranks stable matchings in the same way, although their common ranking is the opposite of the managers' common ranking. Call the matching with $m* = \bar{m}$ the firm-optimal stable matching and the matching with $m* = 0$ the manager-optimal stable matching. Notice from Proposition 4 that $u(\mu(m*), m*)$ is not pinned down when $m* = 0$.

In the firm-optimal stable matching, no firm is hostage to the competitive pressures of the matching market. Every firm matched with $m < \bar{m}$ implements ng, while the rest implement g, the same as what each would do if it could bargain with its matched manager in isolation and had all bargaining power. Compared to this, the manager-optimal stable matching displays widespread pay escalation. Every firm shifts to the rule g, purely because of competition for expensive managerial talent, which results in lower shareholder value. Every type of manager is better off, either because of regime shifts in their own firms, or because of the widespread pay escalation, or both. The total pie is also higher in each match of the manager-optimal stable matching.

Our next result shows that the multiplicity of stable matchings in a balanced market gives way to a unique stable matching, for any imbalance in the measures of potential firms and managers.

*Proposition 5*

*(i) Assume there are more firms than managers ($\gamma > 1$). The unique stable matching is the manager-optimal stable matching of a balanced market with firm distribution $P^+(f) = \max\{0, P(f) – (\gamma − 1)\}$ and manager distribution Q, with a measure $\gamma − 1$ of the lowest quality firms remaining unmatched.*

*(ii) Assume there are fewer firms than managers ($\gamma < 1$). The unique stable matching is the firm-optimal stable matching of a balanced market with firm distribution P and manager distribution $Q^+(m) = \max\{0, Q(m) – (1 − \gamma)\}$, with a measure $1 - \gamma$ of the least talented managers remaining unmatched.*

Proposition 5 is intuitive. When $\gamma > 1$ and there is a shortage of potential managers, the unique equilibrium is the manager-optimal stable matching of the balanced market in which a measure $\gamma - 1$ of the lowest quality firms have effectively been removed from the market. All managers and an equal measure of the highest quality firms match assortatively and implement g. The measure $\gamma - 1$ of the (removed) lowest quality firms remains unmatched. The lowest type of manager earns an expected payoff of $\pi_g(\mu(0), 0) - v_0$ that leaves its match indifferent between matching or staying unmatched. All higher types earn strictly higher payoffs according to (11). In contrast, when $\gamma < 1$ and there is an excess of potential managers, the unique equilibrium is the firm-optimal stable matching of the balanced market in which a measure $1 - \gamma$ of the least talented managers have

[Page 19]
been removed from the market to remain unmatched.²² Sufficiently high quality firms hire talented
managers and implement g, while others implement ng.

As discussed in the Introduction (see also Section 4), technological progress, financial market
innovations, and regulatory reforms may have reduced historical barriers to firm formation. But
high costs of education and training, or other social constraints, may have prevented a corresponding
growth in the number of talented managers. If so, Proposition 5 predicts a sharp escalation in
executive compensation over time, accompanied by changes in firm characteristics due to regime
shifts.

What would happen for fixed sizes, 1 and $\gamma$, of the two sides of the market, but an improvement
in the distribution of types on one side? We focus on distribution improvements in the sense of
strict first-order stochastic dominance. Thus, $P'$ is an improvement over $P$ if $P'(f) < P(f)$ for each
$f \in (0, \bar{f})$; and similarly for the distribution $Q$ of managerial talent. We say that a distribution
change benefits a particular side of the market (manager or firm), if some agents on that side are
strictly better off and no one is strictly worse off. Similarly, the change harms a side of the market
if some agents on that side are strictly worse off and no one is strictly better off.

**Proposition 6** *Consider an unbalanced market ($\gamma \neq 1$). An improvement in the distribution of
one side of the market harms the same side and benefits the opposite side.*

Proposition 6 is a consequence of non-decreasing returns to managerial talent, which is a property of
stable matchings. It is easiest to see for an improvement in the distribution $P$ of firm qualities in a
market with $\gamma > 1$. Because of the shortage of managers, the unique equilibrium before the change
is the manager-optimal stable matching described by Proposition 5. After the change, measure
consistency implies each $m > m^* = 0$ is now matched with a better quality firm than before. So
each manager earns higher returns to managerial talent, as can be seen from expression (11). By
the same token, each matched firm is now matched to a less talented manager, implying the total
pie is smaller in its match. Proposition 6 is of a piece with our preceding results, because it shows
that an improvement over time in the quality distribution of firms, or a worsening of the talent
distribution of managers, may have contributed to the observed escalation in executive pay.²³

We turn next to considering a different force that can generate managerial pay escalation, even
in markets where there are more potential managers than firms, $\gamma < 1$. We ask what would happen
if the type-independent managerial outside option $u_0$ from staying unmatched, assumed so far to

Fix $\gamma < 1$, and for each $m \in [Q^{-1}(1 - \gamma), \bar{m}]$, define the (positively assortative) matching rule $\mu^+(m) = P^{-1}[Q(m) - (1 - \gamma)]$. Let $\phi^+(m) := \phi(\mu^+(m), m)$. Pick $u_0 \in (0, \omega_g)$ and define $m^*_{u_0}$ implicitly as the value of $m$ that solves the equation $\phi^+(m) = \phi^*(u_0)$. Using Figure 4(a), notice that $m^*_{u_0} = \bar{m}$ for $u_0 \le \omega_{ng}$; and $m^*_{u_0} = Q^{-1}(1 - \gamma)$ for $u_0 \ge \bar{u}_0$, where $\bar{u}_0$ solves $\phi(0, Q^{-1}(1-\gamma)) = \phi^*(\bar{u}_0)$, and $\bar{u}_0 < \omega_g$ by R1. Each $u_0$ defines a cutoff type of manager, $m^*_{u_0}$, whose assortatively matched firm $\mu^+(m^*_{u_0})$ is indifferent between regimes.

**Proposition 7** *Suppose there are more managers than firms ($\gamma < 1$). Fix a managerial outside option $u_0 \in (0, \omega_g)$. Then, in the unique stable matching, each $m < m^*_{u_0}$ either stays unmatched for expected payoff $u_0$, or matches with some firm $f \in [0, \mu^+(m^*_{u_0}))$ to implement **ng** for expected payoff $\max\{u_0, \omega_{ng}\}$; while each $m \ge m^*_{u_0}$, matches with $\mu^+(m)$ to implement **g** for expected payoff*
$$
u(\mu^+(m), m) = \omega_g + \kappa \int_{m^*_{u_0}}^m \phi_2(\mu^+(t), t)dt.
$$

Proposition 7 shows it is not necessary to have a relative scarcity of potential managers ($\gamma > 1$) to see escalations in executive pay. A sufficient rise in the outside option of managers is enough to generate such an outcome. In the unique equilibrium, all managers $m < m^*_{u_0}$ either implement **ng** in their matches or are unmatched. Each $m \ge m^*_{u_0}$ earns discontinuously higher payoffs, and increasing returns to talent, implementing **g** in a match with $\mu^+(m)$. Their matched firms also

Panel (b) of Figure 4 depicts (managerial payoffs in) the unique equilibrium for three different cases, corresponding to three possible values of $u_o$. As can be seen from the figure, if $u_o$ rises, then $\bar{m}$ must fall, raising the size of the sector that pursues growth, as well as managerial pay at all levels of talent. When $u_o$ crosses $\bar{u}_o < w_g$, this cascade of regime changes leads all matched pairs to choose g. This extreme case resembles the manager-optimal stable matching of the balanced market, except that the least talented matched manager now earns a lower amount $w_g$. We call this the *minmax* stable matching because among the stable matchings (of the balanced market) that deliver the maximum total pie, it does so at minimum managerial pay.²⁵

A uniform increase in the outside option of all types of managers is not necessary for Proposition 7. As we show in the proof, what is necessary (and sufficient) is that a large enough set (of measure greater than $1 - \gamma$) of the least talented managers below $\bar{m}$ have the higher outside option. This effectively creates a shortage in the supply of relatively less talented managers who are also *cheap enough* for their matched firms to prefer the regime ng. Consequently, some firms must switch to g to clear the market, resulting in pay escalation across the economy, even though there are available managers who remain unmatched. The proximate effect of $u_o$ is on managerial pay for the low risk firms in regime ng, which leads to a spillover effect on the set of high risk firms in regime g. Thus, if an econometrician only observed the subsample of risky firms with high incentive pay, it may not be easily apparent why a rise in $u_o$ affects anything, because each manager in his sample may have expected pay that is an order of magnitude greater than $u_o$.

# 4 Discussion

The post-1980 period marked a sharp departure from the relative stability of the postwar era. Technological advances, major regulatory changes—such as market liberalization, airline deregulation, and telecommunication reforms—and new financial dynamics intensified competition and triggered widespread restructuring (Drach and Cassis 2021; Derthick and Quirk 1985). These forces increased the premium on portable managerial skills and reshaped compensation practices, with

---
²⁴ This equilibrium resembles an interior equilibrium of the balanced market described by Proposition 4, although formally it is the firm-optimal stable matching of the balanced market created by leaving the excess mass of managers unmatched, and taking into account the higher outside option $u_o$.

²⁵ Notice that all managers earn a strictly positive premium over their outside option in this equilibrium as long as $\bar{u}_o < u_o < w_g$. As $u_o$ crosses $w_g$, this premium vanishes. As $u_o$ becomes still larger, managerial payoffs approach that from the manager-optimal stable matching. At the other extreme, for $u_o \le w_{ng}$, the equilibrium is identical to that of Proposition 5(ii).

[Page 22]
highly mobile, high-stakes decision makers commanding extremely large pay packages.²⁶ High-
powered, equity-linked incentives that motivated managers to pursue ambitious but risky growth
opportunities became more common.²⁷ The rise of private equity further strengthened managers’
outside options (Nicholas and Masko 2021).

Our model is consistent with these developments. It has two ingredients: the contracting
problem of Section 2, embedded in the matching market of Section 3. In the contracting problem,
a standard hidden action environment is enriched with hidden information: shareholders are unsure
which actions the manager can actually take. This single friction creates the possibility of regime
shifts, with discontinuous and simultaneous jumps in incentive pay and firm volatility, and a loose
link between pay and realized performance. The frictionless matching market features two-sided
vertical differentiation—firms differ in quality, managers differ in talent—and managerial skills are
portable across firms. Competitive forces then pin down the division of surplus, with no role for
within-firm bargaining power. The interaction of hidden information and competitive matching
generates our results on compensation patterns.

In equilibrium, the highest-quality firms must match with the most talented managers. For
some of these firms, inducing risky growth through high-powered incentives is a dominant strategy.
When technological or regulatory changes shift the distribution of opportunities upward, the set
of firms that prefer the growth regime expands. Lower quality firms paired with less talented
managers face an additional tension: while passing on growth opportunities is optimal for them
when managers are cheap, market-driven wage escalation can make inducing growth attractive,
even when such opportunities are very unlikely to materialize. This feedback loop—market wages
influencing firm regime choice leading to significantly higher incentive pay at most firms—plays a
central role in the empirical implications discussed below.

### Discontinuous jumps in compensation and volatility.
We predict large, abrupt increases
in CEO pay concentrated in firms that move from safe, low-growth strategies to risky, high-growth
ones. Smooth changes in economic conditions produce a sharp jump in incentive pay at the
(marginal) firm that switches regimes. Hidden information frictions are central here: they make
high expected pay unavoidable for firms choosing risky growth, regardless of the actual likelihood
of good opportunities. Without hidden information, no such regime shifts would occur.²⁸

---
²⁶ For example, an annual compensation of $40–50 million is not unusual for a firm such as PayPal. The lifetime
value of such pay may reach half to one billion dollars, a nontrivial fraction of firm value.

²⁷ Stock options became widespread by the 1980s, later supplemented or replaced by performance-based restricted
stock; see Bebchuk and Fried (2004).

²⁸ We show this in Appendix B, where we introduce clawback provisions on managerial compensation contracts.
Such provisions can reduce or eliminate the cost of hidden information. But they may be crowded out of use simply
by high equilibrium levels of incentive pay.

[Page 23]
The model also predicts pay-volatility co-movement. Firms that adopt the growth regime must
offer strong incentives, and these incentives generate greater operating risk. High compensation
and high volatility therefore arise together. This provides a causal explanation for the empirical
association between CEO pay and stock-return volatility (Edmans, Gabaix, and Jenter 2017).
Importantly, these patterns do not rely on governance failures and rent seeking. Regime shifts in
competitive labor markets are sufficient.²⁹

**Disconnect between compensation and shareholder value.** The model yields two implications for the relationship between pay and shareholder value, one arising out of our contracting
problem and the other out of the matching market.

First, within the growth regime, movements in firm value can diverge from changes in the market value of the compensation package. A high-pay contract may simply reflect the need to induce
actions that a manager would otherwise be reluctant to undertake, rather than a prediction of realized shareholder returns. This helps explain the weak or asymmetric pay-performance sensitivities
documented in the empirical literature.³⁰

Second, when a firm switches regimes, shareholder value may move in either direction. If
the switch reflects improved growth opportunities, value rises; if it is instead triggered by wage
escalation in the managerial labor market, value falls. Thus, news that lowers shareholder value
may coincide with higher compensation, a pattern difficult to reconcile with standard contracting
models. Notice that neither version of the disconnect between shareholder value and compensation
implies wasteful rent seeking, since the total pie—shareholder value plus expected compensation
and private benefits—is larger in the growth regime.

**Broad-based pay escalation.** The portability of managerial skills across firms is sufficient
to generate the implications above. Both sets of implications will obtain in our model even when
managers are completely undifferentiated in talent and therefore perfectly portable across firms.
The feedback loop, that can push lower quality firms into the growth regime once market wages
rise, continues to operate even without any differences among managers.

With vertical differentiation in talent, this pattern is amplified: firms in the growth regime
pay more for more talented managers, generating rising compensation as a return to talent—the
superstar effect documented by Edmans, Gabaix and Jenter (2017). Our model accommodates this
channel. But it also shows competition can drive large compensation increases even for managers
of modest ability.

Related phenomena arise when outside options of managers improve. This creates a relative

---
²⁹Contrast with explanations based on learning about managerial ability, governance failures, or institutional
distortions; see Hermalin and Weisbach (1998), Acharya and Volpin (2010), and Acharya, Pagano, and Volpin (2016).

³⁰Aggarwal and Samwick (1999); Bizjak et al. (1993); Yermack (1995).

[Page 24]
scarcity of less talented managers for lower quality firms, leading to more regime shifts and higher
compensation at all talent levels, even when potential managers are in excess supply. Gompers,
Kaplan, and Mukharlyov (2024) document that CEOs hired by private equity between 2010 and
2016 earned unusually high pay, increasing outside options for managers in public firms. Similar
dynamics were likely present during earlier waves of private-equity expansion (Leleux, de Swaay,
and Megally 2015).

**Cascade effects and managerial portability.** Because managerial talent is portable in
our model, competitive pressures can propagate wage increases across the economy. Shocks in
one sector—deregulation, technological change, or tightening labor supply—can trigger cascades
in which many firms adopt high-powered incentive contracts.31 In contrast, when portability is
limited—such as when managers are horizontally differentiated with firm-specific skills—regime
shifts can still take place within a firm. But they are less likely to propagate across firms.

Research suggests that the portability of managerial skills has risen substantially since the 1960s.
Standardized managerial practices, multidivisional structures, and the expansion of management
education increased the extent to which managerial ability operates as general, rather than firm-
specific, human capital (Gordon and Howell 1959; Chandler 1977, 1990; Khurana 2007). The
leveraged-buyout wave of the 1980s further reinforced this shift by promoting the widespread use of
standardized, cash-flow-based performance metrics for evaluating managers (Jensen 1989; Kaplan
1989; Kaplan and Strömberg 2009). Empirical evidence on “generalist” managers confirms rising
mobility and compensation premia (Custódio, Ferreira and Matos 2013, 2019). Viewed through
this lens, the stability of CEO pay prior to 1975, and its rapid escalation afterward, may reflect the
rise in the portability of managerial talent.

Observed succession patterns also align with simple dynamic extensions of our frictionless
matching framework with portable managerial skills. Suppose firms are long-lived but managers
live for two periods, able to serve as CEO in the second period only if they have worked as an
“understudy” to a CEO, either at the same firm or at another, in the first period of their lives.
Firms participate in two separate matching markets, one for understudies and one for CEOs. A
positively assortative stable matching in the understudy market will then automatically induce a
stable matching in next period’s market for CEOs, one in which each firm hires its own under-
study as the CEO. Empirically, roughly 80% of new CEOs are insiders, and external hires often
have close ties to incumbent leadership (Cziraki and Jenter 2022; Quigley and Hambrick 2020),
consistent with firms grooming successors with similar equilibrium levels of portable talent.32

# 5 Conclusion

We develop a competitive matching model for CEOs and firms that incorporates hidden action and
hidden information frictions. Our framework shows how regime shifts in executive compensation
are jointly determined with changes in firm risk profiles, explaining dramatic pay escalation post-
1980, and providing additional sharp testable implications. It also clarifies how high pay can result
from competitive forces even with an excess supply of managers, and why we may see large CEO
compensation even when the firm is struggling.

It is an interesting question whether the well-documented rise in executive compensation levels
is better explained by a race among firms for scarce managerial talent (superstars), or as a regime
shift common to most firms that leads to higher executive compensation associated with firms
choosing high-risk business strategies. We allow for both forces in our model. We find that the
effect of dispersed managerial talent on average executive compensation may well be small - after
all, everyone is unlikely to be a superstar - compared to the effect of a large majority of firms
switching from a low-risk no-growth regime to a high-risk surplus maximizing growth regime that
requires high-powered incentives. Most CEOs may earn high compensation even when they are not
superstars.

The evolution of CEO compensation is closely linked to fundamental shifts in global business.
Understanding the interaction between informational frictions and cascade effects can help explain
compensation dynamics that might otherwise appear baffling or unfair. Beyond direct competi-
tion for executives, cascades may also arise from product-market externalities generated by firms'
regime changes. Our model provides a workhorse framework for analyzing such network effects. As
industries transform rapidly—most notably with advances in AI-aggregate and industry-specific
shocks can trigger regime changes via the mechanisms we study.[^33] The same mechanisms can help
us understand episodes such as the dot-com boom and bust, and shifts in political support for
climate-change abatement.

# A Proofs

## A.1 Proofs for Section 2

### Proof of Proposition 1.

Notice first from (3) that $W_2 > 0$ since $p_G > p_B$ and $b_G < b_B$. Since $b_B > 0$ and $w_2 \ge 0$, this implies from (5) that $w_1 > 0$. Furthermore, (4) and (5) imply (3) and so we ignore this redundant constraint (3) in what follows.

Notice next that either the incentive constraint (4) or the participation constraint (6) must bind at the optimum — for otherwise one can lower the bonus $W_2 > 0$ slightly without violating any constraints and lower the wage bill. Similarly, either the incentive constraint (5) or the participation constraint (6) must bind at the optimum, for otherwise one can lower $w_1 > 0$ slightly without violating any constraints and lowering the wage bill.

Consider first the case where the participation constraint (6) does not bind. Then (4) and (5) must both bind. Letting $\Delta p = p_G - p_B$ and solving these two equations we obtain
$$
W_2 = \frac{b_B - b_G}{\Delta p}; \quad w_1 = w_g + w_2; \quad w_2 = 0
\quad (13)
$$
where we must have $w_2 = 0$ since otherwise from (2) we see that one can lower $w_2$ (and commensurately $w_1$) and lower the wage bill. Substituting the values obtained in 13) into (6), which does not bind by assumption, we must have after some simplification $w_g > u$. This is a necessary condition on $u$ for the values in expression (13) to be the optimal contract. For sufficiency, note that when $u < w_g$, the optimal contract must have $w_1 = w_g$, $w_2 = 0$ and $W_2 = \frac{b_B - b_G}{\Delta p}$ since this is the optimal contract ignoring the participation constraint, which also satisfies the omitted constraint. Finally, the expected wage bill in this case is
$$
\phi(w_2 + p_G W_2) + (1 - \phi)(w_1) = w_g - \phi b_G
\quad (14)
$$

Consider next the remaining case where $u \ge w_g$ and the participation constraint (6) binds. Substituting this constraint, which holds as an equality in the objective function (2), we see that the objective function is equal to $1 + \phi(V_G - 1) - (u - \phi b_G)$, which is independent of the endogenous variables. It follows that any set of $w_1$, $w_2$ and $W_2$ that satisfies the remaining constraints is an optimal contract. One such optimal contract (for which the incentive constraints bind) has
$$
W_2 = \frac{b_B - b_G}{\Delta p}; \quad w_2 = u - w_g; \quad w_1 = u.
\quad (15)
$$
In this case, the expected wage bill is
$$
\phi(w_2 + p_G W_2) + (1 - \phi)(w_1) = u - \phi b_G
\quad (16)
$$

[Page 27]
Comparing (14) and (16), we see that the wage bill can be written as $\max \{u,w_g\} – \phi_G b_G$; and, using (13) and (15), the optimal contract can be written as
$$
w_1 = \max \{u, w_g\} \,; \quad w_2 = \max \{0, u - w_g\}\,; \quad W_2 = \frac{b_B - b_g}{\Delta p}
$$
This completes the proof.
■

**Proof of Proposition 2.**

When $G$ does not arrive, the market value of the firm at date 1, net of managerial compensation equals $1 - w_1$ where $w_1 = \max\{u, w_g\}$, using Proposition 1. Instead, when $G$ arrives and the manager chooses it, the market value of the firm at date 1, net of managerial compensation equals $V_G - (w_2 + p_G W_2)$ where, again using Proposition 1,
$$
w_2 + p_G W_2 = \max \{0, u - w_g\} + p_G \frac{b_B - b_g}{\Delta p}
$$
We claim first that $w_1$ is greater, equal or less than $w_2 + p_G W_2$ according as $b_G$ is positive, zero, or negative. If $u \ge w_g$, from above one obtains $w_1 = u$ and $w_2 + p_G W_2 = u - b_G$. Instead, when $u < w_g$, one obtains $w_1 = w_g$ and $w_2 + p_G W_2 = \frac{p_G b_B - p_G b_G}{\Delta p}$, and so in either case the claim follows. We show next that $1 - w_1 < V_G - (w_2+p_G W_2)$. Since $V_G > 1$, this is immediate if $w_1 \ge w_2+p_G W_2$ which obtains whenever $b_G \ge 0$. On the other hand, when $b_G < 0$, using the expressions for $w_1$ and $w_2 + p_G W_2$ obtained above, it is also easy to verify that the same inequality is guaranteed, using (1).

Let $\phi_\psi = \Pr[G \text{ arrives}|\psi]$. Because $\psi$ is an informative signal, in particular (8), we must have $\phi_H > \phi_L$. Furthermore,
$$
P_{1,\psi} = \phi_\psi (V_G - (w_2 + p_G W_2)) + (1 - \phi_\psi) (1 - w_1), \text{ and,}
$$
$$
E [W|\psi] = \phi_\psi (V_G - (w_2 + p_G W_2)) + (1 - \phi_\psi) (1 - w_1).
$$
Since $\phi_H > \phi_L$, we conclude $P_{1,H} > P_{1,L}$ and $E [W|H] < E[W|L]$ iff $b_G > 0$. The result now follows.
■

**Proof of Proposition 3.** From the statement of Proposition 1, shareholders get a payoff of $1 + \phi_G(V_G - 1 + b_G) - \max\{u,w_g\}$ when they induce g. From the discussion preceding the statement of the proposition, shareholders get a payoff of $1 - \max\{u, w_{ng}\}$ when they induce ng. The proposition is a straightforward comparison of these two payoffs.

**Definition 1** Given a matching $(\mu, \theta, u, v)$, we define the following:
$X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} := \{m \in [0, \bar{m}] : \theta(\mu(m), m) = \mathrm{g}\}$, $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}} := \{m \in [0, \bar{m}] : \theta(\mu(m), m) = \mathrm{ng}\}$,
$u_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} := \inf\{u(\mu(m), m) : m \in X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}}\}$, $m_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} := \inf X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}}$; $f_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} := \inf \mu(X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}})$.

**Proof of Proposition 4.** Suppose that $\gamma = 1$ and $(\mu, \theta, u, v)$ is a stable matching. We first note that in a balanced market, all firms and managers must be matched, for if $m$ is an unmatched manager and $f$ an unmatched firm, then, $(f, m)$ can block $(\mu, \theta, u, v)$ by implementing $\theta = \mathrm{ng}$.

We start with the "only if" part. We observe that $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} \neq \emptyset$ since $\underline{m} < \bar{m}$, a consequence of R1. Given that $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} \neq \emptyset$, Lemma 4 tells us that there exists a cutoff $m^* \in [0, \bar{m}]$ such that the support of $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} = [m^*, \bar{m}]$ and the support of $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}} = [0, m^*]$. We note that given a cutoff $m^*$, either, $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} = (m^*, \bar{m}]$, $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}} = [0, m^*]$ or $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} = [m^*, \bar{m}]$, $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}} = [0, m^*)$. Wlog, going forward we assume that $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} = [m^*, \bar{m}]$, $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}} = [0, m^*)$, that is, $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}}$ is closed. The other case is handled identically.

Furthermore, Lemma 2 tells us that $\mu$ is positively assortative on $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}}$ and therefore given by $\mu(m) = P^{-1}Q(m)$ whenever $m \in X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}}$ since $\mu$ is measure-consistent. Note that $\mu$ is indeterminate on $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}}$ since manager talent and firm quality does not matter when implementing $\mathrm{ng}$ and therefore, $\mu(m) \in [0, P^{-1}Q(m^*)]$ whenever $m \in X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}}$.

Suppose that $m^* > \underline{m}$, then, from the continuity of $\phi$ we know that $\phi(\mu(m^*) - \epsilon, m^* - \epsilon) > \phi^*(0)$ for some $\epsilon > 0$. This means that manager $\mu(m^*) - \epsilon$ and the firm $m^* - \epsilon$ are better off by inducing $\mathrm{g}$ and therefore block $(\mu, \theta, u, v)$. This shows that $m^* \in [0, \bar{m}]$. We further know from Lemma 5 that the payoff of a manager $m$ in $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}}$ is given by $u(\mu(m), m) = u_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} + (V_G - 1 + b_G) \int_{m^*}^m \phi_2(\mu(t), t)dt$. To continue, we split the rest of the argument into two cases

(i) $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}} \neq \emptyset$. This corresponds to the case when $m^* > 0$. We claim that $u_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}} = \omega_g$. To see this, first note that from Proposition 1 we know that $\omega_g$ is the lowest utility that needs to be offered to any manager in $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}}$. Next note that if $u_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}} > \omega_g$, then, since $\phi, \mu$ and $u$ are all continuous, we can find a manager $m^* - \epsilon$ and a firm $\mu(m^* + \epsilon)$ that will block the matching by choosing $\epsilon$ sufficiently small because the payoff of the manager $m^* - \epsilon$ in the matching is given by $u_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}}$ and we know from Lemma 6 that $u_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} - u_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}} = \phi(\mu(m^*), m^*)(V_G - 1 + b_G) > 0$.

(ii) $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{ng}} = \emptyset$. This corresponds to the case when $m^* = 0$. We claim that that $u_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} \in [\omega_g, \pi_g(0,0) - v_0]$. Note that in this case $X_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} = [0, \bar{m}]$. It is clear from the preceding arguments that $u_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} \ge \omega_g$. Further notice that $u_{\mu}^{\mathrm{g}} \le \pi_g(0,0) - v_0$, otherwise firm of quality 0 prefers to stay unmatched.

This concludes the proof of the "only if" part.

To see the “if” part, given a matching $(\mu, \theta, u, v)$ and the corresponding $m^* \in [0, \bar{m}]$, we consider a candidate blocking coalition $(f, m)$. We first show that if $f = \mu(m)$, then $(f, m)$ will not block,

[Page 29]
either by implementing a different decision rule or by proposing a different split or both. To see this, observe that the only possibility where $(\mu(m), m)$ can block is when $\theta(\mu(m), m) = \text{ng}$ as this is the only case when the total surplus in a given match can possibly increase by implementing the decision rule g instead of ng. Therefore, $m < m^*$ and $\mu(m) < \mu(m^*)$. Note that the minimum utility of m if g is implemented is given by $\omega_g$. The minimum increase in utility of m when implementing g as opposed to ng is equal to $\phi(\mu(m^*),m^*)(V_G - 1 + b_G)$, however, the gain in total surplus is equal to $\phi(\mu(m),m)(V_G - 1 + b_G) < \phi(\mu(m^*), m^*)(V_G - 1 + b_G)$ since $m < m^*$ and $\mu(m) < \mu(m^*)$. Therefore, $(f, m)$ cannot block.

Next we show that when $f \neq \mu(m)$, $(f,m)$ will not block. There are four possibilities for a candidate blocking coalition $(f, m)$, we consider each separately below:

(i) $f \in \mu(X_g^f)$ and $m \in X_g^m$. Consider the case when $f > \mu(m)$. This implies that there exists $m_1 \in X_g^m$ such that $f = \mu(m_1)$. Clearly, $(f,m)$ cannot block if $\theta(f,m) = \text{ng}$ as the total surplus generated in any pair inducing ng is lower than the total surplus generated in any pair inducing g. Therefore, suppose that $\theta(f,m) = \text{g}$ and $u(f,m) = u(\mu(m), m)$. The utility of f in the matching is $v(f, m_1) = 1 + \phi(f, m_1)(V_G - 1 + b_G) - u(f, m_1)$. The utility of f in the candidate blocking coalition is $v(f, m) = 1 + \phi(f,m)(V_G - 1 + b_G) - u(f, m)$. Note that
$v(f, m) - v(f, m_1) = (V_G - 1 + b_G)(\phi(f,m) - \phi(f, m_1)) + u(f, m_1) - u(f,m)$
$= (V_G - 1 + b_G)(\phi(f,m) - \phi(f,m_1)) + u(\mu(m_1), m_1) - u(\mu(m),m)$
$= (V_G - 1 + b_G)(\phi(f,m) - \phi(f, m_1)) + (V_G - 1 + b_G) \int_{m}^{m_1} \phi_2(\mu(t), t)dt$
$= (V_G - 1 + b_G) \int_{m}^{m_1} (\phi_2(\mu(t), t) - \phi_2(f,t))dt$.

Since $\phi$ is supermodular and $f > \mu(t)$ for all $t \in (m, m_1)$, the integrand in the above expression $\phi_2(\mu(t), t) - \phi_2(f,t) \le 0$, and, therefore, $v(f,m) - v(f,m_1) \le 0$ while keeping $u(f,m) = u(\mu(m), m)$ and therefore $(f, m)$ cannot block. Using an analogous argument, we can extend this result to the case when $f < \mu(m)$ and therefore we omit it.

(ii) $f \in \mu(X_{ng}^f)$ and $m \in X_{ng}^m$. Suppose that $\theta(f,m) = \text{ng}$, and $u(f,m) = u(\mu(m), m)$. Let $f = \mu(m_1)$. The utility of f in the matching is $v(f, m_1) = 1+\phi(f,m_1)(V_G-1+b_G)-u(f,m_1)$.
